coredata.dc:description: Environmental remediation of atrazine, being a widely used herbicide from wastewater poses significant environmental challenges. The present investigation reports on the development of new approach to enhance photocatalytic degradation of atrazine by integrating a 3D-printed Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs nanocatalyst with BiVO4 semiconductor. Such synergistic combination could promote interfacial charge transfer processes, thereby improving the efficiency of the photocatalytic system. Mo2CTx, a member of MXene family, has outstanding conductivity and a large surface area, making it a potential candidate for increasing charge transport and catalytic performance. UiO-66, a metal-organic framework provides stable and porous scaffold for anchoring Mo2CTx and rGQDs (reduced graphene quantum dots) to enhance catalytic efficiency. The incorporation of BiVO4 semiconductor into the nanocomposite facilitated light absorption to extend photocatalytic activity in the visible spectrum. The 3D printed composite showed efficient atrazine degradation up to 98.19 % under the irradiation time of 120 min offering the reaction rate of 0.03135 min−1. This is accompanied by excellent recyclability of the 3D printed composite compared to the traditional powder samples. The mechanism and degradation pathway of atrazine photocatalytic process was evaluated quantitatively.

head.ce:title: Integrating 3D-printed Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs nanocatalysts with semiconducting BiVO4 to improve interfacial charge transfer and photocatalytic degradation of atrazine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental remediation of atrazine, being a widely used herbicide from wastewater poses significant environmental challenges. The present investigation reports on the development of new approach to enhance photocatalytic degradation of atrazine by integrating a 3D-printed Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs nanocatalyst with BiVO4 semiconductor. Such synergistic combination could promote interfacial charge transfer processes, thereby improving the efficiency of the photocatalytic system. Mo2CTx, a member of MXene family, has outstanding conductivity and a large surface area, making it a potential candidate for increasing charge transport and catalytic performance. UiO-66, a metal-organic framework provides stable and porous scaffold for anchoring Mo2CTx and rGQDs (reduced graphene quantum dots) to enhance catalytic efficiency. The incorporation of BiVO4 semiconductor into the nanocomposite facilitated light absorption to extend photocatalytic activity in the visible spectrum. The 3D printed composite showed efficient atrazine degradation up to 98.19 % under the irradiation time of 120 min offering the reaction rate of 0.03135 min−1. This is accompanied by excellent recyclability of the 3D printed composite compared to the traditional powder samples. The mechanism and degradation pathway of atrazine photocatalytic process was evaluated quantitatively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 3D-printed-Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4 composites showed efficient atrazine degradation. The mechanism and degradation pathway of the atrazine photocatalytic process was also proposed in this study.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Atrazine has been the widely employed herbicide that is frequently combined with other ingredients in agriculture sectors [1,2] to remove weeds in crops [3], causing groundwater pollution [4]. Given the hazardous nature of atrazine, a broad range of conventional and advanced techniques have been attempted to achieve its degradation to reduce the toxicity levels of atrazine from wastewater sources [5]. The most commonly used techniques such as adsorption and chemical degradation have often met with drawbacks such as incomplete degradation, secondary pollutant formation, or high energy consumption to offer cleaner water [6]. Photocatalysis, on the other hand, being a low-cost [7] and renewable method uses light energy to eliminate harmful pollutants by converting them into chemical energy [8]. The UV light absorption approach includes generating electron-hole pairs, reducing the valence band (VB) and oxidizing the conduction band (CB) [9]. The progress in photocatalysis field has resulted to create highly effective photocatalytic system for efficiently degrading atrazine [10].

Several widely explored photocatalysts such as TiO2, ZnO, and Sb2O3 have been employed due to their notable oxidation capacity, chemical stability, non-toxic nature, and cost-effectiveness [11], but these catalysts exhibit optimal performance solely under the radiation of UV light. For instance, TiO2 with an absorption peak at around 390 nm, utilizes only 4 % of the sunlight with its wide band gap of 3.2 eV, facilitating electron-hole recombination, thereby limiting the effective charge separation [12]. Among the recent catalysts employed, bismuth-based oxides such as BiVO4, Bi2WO6, Bi2MoO6, and Bi4Ti3O12 have emerged as the promising candidates. Recent study highlighted the efficacy Bi2WO6's in degrading Rhodamine B and methyl orange offering the degradation efficiencies of 97 % and 92 %, respectively [13]. Similarly, Bi2MoO6/Bi/TiO2 composites achieved 82.73 % decomposition rate of tetracycline under the visible light within 120 min [14]. These outcomes are attributed to synergistic interaction of the metal and oxygen [15]. Notably, BiVO4 garners much attention because of its low band gap (around 2.4–2.5 eV), enabling the effective visible light absorption, excellent dispersion, non-toxicity, corrosion resistance, and remarkable efficacy in decomposing organic pollutants using multijunction heterostructured materials [16] under the visible light [17]. However, more efforts are needed to address the strategies including doping and co-catalyst loading to form composites with other materials to enhance charge separation and extend the light absorption capacity.

In efforts to enhance the catalytic efficacy of semiconductors, recent research focused on integrating MXene materials with semiconductors. This integration can significantly improve the photovoltaic performance by providing substantial activity and reducing the photochemical charge recombination. MXenes offer the advantage of tunable bandgaps ranging from 0.92 to 1.75 eV, excellent conductivity, and providing a large surface area. For example, TiO2/Ti3C2Tx combination demonstrated 98.67 % decomposition rate for carbamazepine under UV radiation [18]. Mo2CTx@Fe3O4 showed the highest catalytic performance for methylisothiazolinone decomposition with removal rate of 93.41 % and 62.46 % in 120 min, showcasing the potential of MXene-enhanced catalysts [19].

Mo₂CTx MXenes are now emerging as highly promising platforms for photocatalytic removal of pollutants [20] due to their unique structural organization that provides extensive surface area, exposing numerous active sites to the pollutants, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic reactions via nanoconfinements [21] and interfacial engineering [22]. Additionally, the surface terminations on MXenes can be tailored to enhance their photocatalytic activity, while their chemical stability ensures long-term performance in aqueous environments [23]. When combined with other photocatalysts such as Mo2CTx MXenes can form heterojunctions to help further improve optoelectrical properties, effectively degrading a wide range of organic pollutants for environmental remediation.

Reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) are a novel class of quantum dots extensively studied because of their unique quantum properties [24]. The rGQDs exhibit numerous outstanding physicochemical properties, including stable photoluminescence, high thermal conductivity, and enhanced surface grafting capabilities, making them promising materials for optoelectronic devices and are ideal for the loading applications [25]. Moreover, UiO-66 has been recognized as having superior physicochemical properties, though it suffers from low photocatalytic efficiency [26]. However, integrating semiconductors such as CdS, Bi2MoO6, and TiO2 with UiO-66 are known to significantly enhance its performance in photocatalytic removal [27] during wastewater treatment [28]. The study further explores the promising combination of UiO-66 with rGQDs through 3D printing technology. The 3D printing enables intended designs over the structure and distribution of UiO-66 and rGQDs, enhancing their synergistic effects. Furthermore, integrating 3D printing technology allows for precise control over the material architecture, resulting in tailored properties and optimized performance.

In this study, Mo2CTx was prepared and incorporated into a 3D-printed Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs composite with BiVO4 semiconductor. The objective was to improve the overall performance of the photocatalytic system for degrading atrazine in waste aqueous environments. The study investigates atrazine degradation rates, photocatalytic oxidation mechanisms, and quantitative degradation pathways under the Xe lamp irradiation, which mimics the sun's rays (320 nm < λ < 780 nm). The light source simulates the deterioration process occurring under the direct sunlight. Such an investigation could open new windows for practical applications of 3D-printed Mo2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs composites with BiVO4 semiconductors to utilize the sunlight-induced photocatalysis for material reusability and photodegradation of organic pollutants.

3D printing acrylate resin solution was purchased from Carima (Seoul, Korea). Bismuth(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3.5H2O) (≥98 %), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3, ≥99 %), terephthalic acid (H2BDC, 98 %), anhydrous methanol (CH3OH, 99.6 %) and atrazine obtained from Merck (St. Louis USA). All the other chemicals used were of reagent grade samples and deionized (DI) water.

Raman spectroscopy was performed with a Renishaw InVia confocal microscopic system (Wotton-under-Edge, UK). The analysis was conducted at a wavelength of 633 nm, generated by a He-Ne laser operating at 17 mW power. Spectral acquisition lasted for 10 sec as standard. The characterization of nanocomposite morphology, size, and elemental distribution was carried out utilizing microscopic and spectroscopic instruments [8]. The 3D printing was accomplished using a Carima IM-96 printing system employing digital light processing (DLP) technology. Fluorescence imaging of samples was conducted using a Zeiss LSM900 fluorescence microscope equipped with a diode laser. Transient photocurrent measurements were performed using electrochemical workstation (CHI 6005E, CH Instruments Inc.) equipped with a typical three-electrode system. UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) was detected on a Shimadzu integrating sphere-attached UV-3600 spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan), using BaSO4 as the reference. Atrazine and its degradation products were investigated using a GC-MS system (Shimadzu, GC-MS, serial number 021745902943, models GC-2030 and GCMS-QP2020NX) and the DB-5MS UI column. For each measurement, a 10 µL extract was directly injected into the injection port at 280 °C. The MS spectra were recorded over the mass range of 2–300 m/z to identify the decomposition intermediates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Mo2CTx, rGQDs, BiVO4, UiO-66@rGQDs, and Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4
ce:section.ce:para: Mo2CTx MXenes were synthesized using a hydrothermal etching method that were obtained after freeze-drying for 24 h [29]. The rGQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment [30]. A crystal sample of BiVO4 was created via a hydrothermal process [31]. UiO-66@rGQDs crystals were prepared using a mixture of 25 mL of DMF containing ZrCl4 (93.2 mg) and another 25 mL DMF solution of H2BDC (66.4 mg) under ultrasonication at room temperature [32]. A mixture was prepared by adding 250 mg of Mo₂CTₓ and 75 mg of UiO-66@rGQDs dispersed in water and stirring to facilitate an attachment of UiO-66@rGQDs particles onto the surface of Mo2CTx, forming the preliminary mixture to which 250 mg of BiVO4 was added and stirred for 6 h. The mixture was centrifuged and washed with water and ethanol to remove the unbound particles; the resulting product was dried under a vacuum.

The 3D composite printed model (Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4) was fabricated to resemble a gyroid structure with side dimensions of 1 cm. The printing ink was prepared by mixing the powder of the catalyst mixture sample with the resin (Carima Acryl, 3DKBLK), which was thoroughly blended to form a homogeneous solution. This ensures even distribution of the catalyst components within the resin, maintaining consistent photocatalytic performance across the final structure. The DLP type-3D printing system employed a light source (405 nm, 1.97 mW/cm2) to cure the ink. The printed sample consisted of 101 layers with curing times of 10 s for the first layer and 9 s for the subsequent layers. The entire printing process took 34 min to complete. The finished sample was washed several times with ethanol to ensure the complete removal of any residual resin from the surface of the printed model.

An electrochemical workstation (CHI 600D, CH Instruments Inc.) was used to perform the transient photocurrent measurements, in which Pt and Ag/AgCl electrodes were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. For the fabrication of the working electrode, the sample was coated onto fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO), which was used as the working electrode. Na2SO4 (0.5 M) was used as the electrolyte, and the prepared working electrode was irradiated by an Xe lamp equipped with a light on/off cycle of 20 s.

Photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed under the artificial UV light (wavelength 365 nm, Lichtzen, South Korea) and a 300 W Xenon lamp (95 mW/cm2, TOP-X300, Short Arc Xenon Lamp Source, Toption, China). The experiments were conducted in a dark environment to ensure no interference from the external light sources. A 70 mL vial served as the reaction vessel for photocatalytic experiments with a UV lamp/Xenon lamp (300 W) positioned approximately 20 cm above the liquid surface. All the experiments were conducted in ambient air, maintaining a constant temperature of 25 °C.

In experiments involving powder samples, each vial was filled with 50 mL of atrazine solution (5 ppm) and approximately 0.003 g of the catalyst. During the illumination experiments, 2 mL of the contaminated solution was taken and analyzed using the GC-MS system (Shimadzu, GCMS-QP2020NX). For the 3D printed samples, the printed sample was washed with ethanol for 12 h to remove surface contaminants. This washing step ensures the printed sample is cleaned and prepared for subsequent reuse in further photocatalytic reactions. From the GC-MS data, the decomposition efficiency of atrazine in the photocatalytic reaction was calculated as:
(1) Degradation efficiency (%) = (1-Ct/C0) x 100
where C0 is the absorbance of the initial atrazine solution (5 ppm) and Ct is the absorbance of the sample at each time point after the irradiation.

The morphologies of the photocatalyst were assessed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and the Mo2Ga2C sample shown in Fig. 1 a exhibits a typical layered structure. After removing the metal "A" (Ga) in MAX by the Fluorine-Free method (HCl-etching), the morphology of Mo2CTx (Fig. 1 b) did not show any characteristics structure of Mxene, and layers in the stacking have slight expansion such as Nb2CTx [33], Ti3C2Tx [34]. On the basis of elemental mapping data (Fig. 1 e and Figure S1), the Mo2Ga2C sample contains elements like Mo, Ga, and C that are evenly distributed; the signal of element Ga decreased significantly after HCl-etching. This is even more evident in EDS results, which showed that the removal of Ga element from the MAX was successful. Besides, the nanoparticles were also dispersed quite uniformly. Figure S2 shows that the UiO-66@rGQDs sample showed fluorescence, but it was not in the UiO-66 sample, indicating that rGQDs were successfully added to UiO-66. The BiVO4 sample observed under an electron microscope (Fig. 1 c) exhibits a typical dodecahedron shape (500 nm). The top-bottom surfaces and the trapezoid edges are assigned to 010 and 110 faces, respectively [35]. Fig. 2 and Figure S3 show the TEM image of the Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4 composite. The image clearly shows that the octahedral UiO-66@rGQDs and dodecahedral BiVO4 structures are attached around and on the MXene flakes, which is consistent with the SEM data. The corresponding mappings in the HR-TEM image show the relatively uniform dispersion of BiVO4 and UiO-66@rGQDs around and on the surface of MXene.

The composite displayed in Fig. 1 d demonstrates fairly uniform dispersion of UiO-66@rGQDs and BiVO₄ onto the surface of MXene, but the morphology of individual samples remained intact showing no structural damage. Element mapping of 3D printed Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ composite in Fig. 1 e showed relatively uniform dispersion of Mo, V, Bi, and Zr onto the surface of 3D printed composite, indicating that the powder sample was uniformly dispersed in the resin. The elements C and O exhibited better mapping than the other elements, as these two elements are abundant in the resin.

Raman spectrum shows a notable change after etching, indicating the disappearance or shift ( Fig. 3 a). Specifically, in Mo₂Ga₂C, the peaks at 180 and 234 cm⁻¹ represent E₂g and A₁g modes, respectively. These peaks gradually shifted to 141 and 252 cm⁻¹ due to the removal of the Ga layer and subsequent terminations.

Furthermore, the A₁g mode of the Mo₂CTₓ spectrum exhibits a decrease at 316 cm⁻¹ , which is due to the displacement of the Mo atom and the opposite displacement of the nearby Ga atoms. Additionally, the peaks at 488 and 680 cm⁻¹ represent additional E₂g and A₁g modes correlated with the functional groups produced on the surfaces [29]. These peaks of Mo₂CTₓ indicated the successful synthesis of MXene [36]. The Raman spectrum of BiVO4 showed the bending modes of the VO4 tetrahedron [37]. The Raman spectrum of the mixture indicates that the characteristic peaks of BiVO₄ and Mo₂CTₓ remain unchanged, and no new peaks appeared. This suggests that the combination of Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ was successful without any structural destruction.

The XRD pattern for the Mo2Ga2C sample, depicted in Fig. 3 b, reveals primary peaks at 9.66°, 33.99°, 37.26°, 39.74°, 42.37°, 49.47°, 53.31°, and 60.97°. These peaks are associated with the diffraction planes (002), (100), (103), (008), (105), (107), (108), and (110), respectively [38]. The intensity of other peaks associated with the Mo2Ga2C structure significantly decreased. Additionally, the observed shift and broadening of the (002) peak suggest an increase in interlayer distance and an expansion of the lattice parameter c from 17.86 Å to 22 Å due to the Ga removal [39]. The diffraction pattern of the BiVO4 powder sample indicates its monoclinic scheelite (CPDS No. 14–0688), showing no abnormal diffraction peaks and confirming good crystallinity [31], which is consistent with the previous reports [40]. The XRD scattering spectrum of UiO-66-rGQDs was consistent with the previously reported XRD pattern of UiO-66 [8]. The characteristic peaks of UiO-66 were still retained (2θ =7.3° and 8.43°). This further suggests that the rGQDs did not disrupt the connectivity and framework of UiO-66. The diffraction pattern (Fig. 3 d) of the mixed sample showed similarities with Raman scattering. The diffraction peaks of the individual samples are present in the composite sample, indicating that no new crystals were formed and no existing crystals have been destroyed.

The PL spectrum shown in Fig. 3 c demonstrates the effectiveness of charge separation in the catalyst. In BiVO₄ and UiO-66@rGQDs samples, the PL intensity was quite high, indicating that the recombination rate is also fast. In contrast, the Mo₂CTₓ showed an ineffective electron-hole pair separation, causing a poor photocatalytic performance. By combining Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄, the charge separation was significantly improved, enhancing the overall photocatalytic efficiency. To better understand the surface properties of the photocatalytic material, the Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4 sample was analyzed by nitrogen adsorption and desorption measurements (Figure S4), which showed the characteristics of a mesoporous material with the measured BET surface area of 91 m²/g. The curve has a hysteresis loop, which is typical of mesoporous pores, consistent with the structure of the hybrid material. Such a relatively large surface area suggests the ability to provide numerous active sites for adsorption and photocatalytic reactions, supporting the efficient degradation of atrazine. The uniform mesoporous structure improved the diffusion of reactants and products during the photocatalytic process.

XPS spectroscopy was employed to investigate the surface compositions and chemical states of Mo₂CTₓ, UiO-66@rGQDs, BiVO₄, and the Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ composite, as shown in Fig. 4. As observed in Fig. 4 a, the significant reduction of Ga 2p signals (1145.4 and 1117.9 eV) after etching confirms the effective removal of Ga from the surface. The appearance of Cl 2 s and Cl 2p peaks at 269.4 eV and 198.6 eV, respectively, indicates the presence of chlorine groups on the Mo₂CTₓ surface. Furthermore, XPS of Mo2Ga2C and Mo2CTx were measured to analyze the variations in chemical compositions due to fluorine-free etching.

The XPS Mo 3d spectrum of Mo2CTx (Fig. 4 b) was employed to examine the electronic state of Mo atoms. The primary bands at 228.6 and 231.9 eV correlate to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, associated with the C-Mo-Tx species. Notably, the binding energy of 228.6 eV for Mo 3d5/2 is higher than the 228.1 eV reported for Mo-C species in Mo2Ga2C, indicating the loss of Ga bonding in the structure [38]. This change suggests binding with more electronegative surface terminators such as O, OH, and/or Cl [41]. Additionally, four other peaks were identified: Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 at 229.4 and 232.8 eV, corresponding to Mo5+, and bands at 230.7 eV (Mo 3d5/2) and 234.8 eV (Mo 3d3/2), correlated to Mo6+ species [42].

For UiO-66@rGQDs, the XPS Zr 3d spectrum shows two peaks at 182.1 eV and 184.4 eV, correlating with Zr 3d₅/₂ and Zr 3d₃/₂, confirming the Zr⁴⁺ oxidation state within the UiO-66 framework (Fig. 4 c). The retention of these peaks indicates that the structural integrity of UiO-66 is preserved following the incorporation of rGQDs [43]. The C 1 s spectrum of UiO-66@rGQDs exhibits several peaks: at 284 eV (sp3 C–C and C-H bonds), 285.5 eV (sp2 CC bonds), and 287.9 eV (OC-O bonds), characteristic of the graphitic carbon in the reduced graphene quantum dots, consistent with previous studies [44].

The intensity of the C 1 s peaks in the composite compared to the individual UiO-66 suggests that rGQDs are well dispersed within the composite material. For BiVO₄, the Bi 4f spectrum exhibits peaks at 158.6 eV (Bi 4f₇/₂) and 163.9 eV (Bi 4f₅/₂), which correspond to Bi³⁺ in the BiVO₄. The V 2p spectrum reveals two peaks at 516.9 eV (V 2p₃/₂) and 524.1 eV (V 2p₁/₂), which are attributed to V⁵⁺ in the vanadate structure. These binding energies are consistent with the oxidation states expected for BiVO₄ and indicate the stability of the material in the composite [45].

The XPS spectra of Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ composite reveal several significant shifts in binding energies, suggesting strong electronic interactions between the components. The Mo 3d peaks shift slightly to higher binding energies by 0.1–0.2 eV indicating a reduction in electron density around Mo atoms, likely due to charge transfer from Mo₂CTₓ to other components in the UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ composite. Similarly, the Bi 4f and V 2p peaks in the composite also shift slightly to higher binding energies (by ∼0.4–0.5 eV), signifying electronic coupling between BiVO₄ and Mo₂CTₓ and UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄. These shifts in binding energies, particularly in the Mo 3d and Bi 4f regions, signify strong electronic interactions within the composite, which may enhance its catalytic or electronic capacity. The results indicate that the integration of Mo₂CTₓ, UiO-66@rGQDs, and BiVO₄ contributes to significant modifications in the electronic structure, reinforcing the synergistic effects in the composite material.

In this way, combining Mo2CTx with UiO-66@rGQDs as an electron transfer facilitator, and materials with good photovoltaic effects such as BiVO4, which can improve the photocatalytic efficiency of the mixture. The study on the mixing ratio between BiVO4 and the Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs mixture is presented in Fig. 5 b. The photocurrent intensity of 10:3:10 sample Mo2CTx: UiO-66@rGQDs: BiVO4 was found to be the highest. Consequently, this ratio was chosen for conducting photocatalytic reactions and for 3D printing applications.

UV-Vis DRS was introduced to examine the optical absorption characteristics of the samples BiVO4, UiO-66@rGQDs, and the composite sample as shown in Fig. 5 c. The BiVO4 demonstrated good visible light with its absorption edge at approximately 535 nm. The bandgap energy as calculated using the Tauc equation was 2.39 eV (Fig. 5 d). The absorption edge of UiO-66@rGQDs was observed at 345 nm, attributed to its bandgap energy of 3.94 eV. The spectrum of Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4 composite showed enhanced and extended light absorption, indicating that this material can capture more solar energy effectively.

The photodegradation activities of BiVO4, Mo2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄, and 3D-printed Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ under visible light illumination were evaluated, as presented in Fig. 6 a-b. The photocatalysts were stirred in the dark for at least 30 min to reach the reaction equilibrium. During the adsorption stage in the dark, UiO-66@rGQDs demonstrated the properties characteristic of a hollow material, resulting in a prominent decrease in atrazine concentration compared to the other samples. The results in Fig. 6 a show that 3D-printed composite catalyst exhibited better photocatalytic performance showing higher atrazine degradation rate than other catalysts. After 120 min of visible light illumination, the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4, Mo2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄, and 3D-printed Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ were 85.56 %, 80.89 %, 65.81 %, 97.72 %, and 98.19 %, respectively (Fig. 6 b). The rate constants (k) of BiVO4, Mo2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄, and 3D-printed Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ were 0.01396 min−1, 0.01215 min−1, 0.00539 min−1, 0.03044 min−1, and 0.03135 min−1, respectively (Fig. 6 c). The k values for the decomposition of atrazine by the 3D-composites catalyst were approximately 2.3 times higher than that of BiVO4, 2.6 times higher than that of Mo2CTx, and 5.8 times higher than that of UiO-66@rGQDs.

To evaluate the effectiveness of 3D-printed Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ photocatalyst, its first-order reaction rate constant for atrazine degradation (0.03135 min⁻¹) was compared with those reported in the literature for other photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 6c, the rate constant for 3D-printed sample photocatalyst is higher than those of nanoparticulate TiO₂ films (0.0229 min⁻¹) [46] and boron-doped TiO₂ with a tunable rutile/anatase ratio (0.016 min⁻¹) [47], indicating superior efficiency in photocatalytic degradation of atrazine under similar conditions. While Ag₂O/BaFe₁₂O₁₉ nano heterojunction with 2 % Ag₂O/barium hexaferrite [48] demonstrates a slightly higher rate constant (0.039 min⁻¹), the Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ composite remains competitive and offers promising alternative for environmental applications, particularly in atrazine degradation.

The stability and reusability of 3D-composites scaffold in the degradation of atrazine were conducted and monitored for 5 cycles (Fig. 6 d). The 3D-printed samples were cleaned with DI water a few times after each step before performing a new cycle. The results showed that the decomposition efficiency and degradation rate of atrazine were similar for all 5 cycles, indicating good durability and reusability. After undergoing the reuse processes, XRD analysis was used to evaluate the structural changes of the catalyst materials. Figure S5 and Figure S6 present the XRD spectrum and actual images of the 3D composites before and after the photocatalytic process, respectively. The results showed no significant changes after multiple reuses, indicating that the 3D-printed samples had good stability. The 3D photocatalyst samples are structurally more stable than powder samples, and they can be recycled and reused multiple times with only minor surface cleaning or processing, demonstrating the outstanding benefits of incorporating 3D printing in photocatalytic applications.

The photocatalytic degradation of BiVO4, UiO-66@rGQDs, and 3D-composite samples was investigated using the atrazine decomposition under UV irradiation as shown in Fig. 7. Initially, atrazine and the photocatalysts reached adsorption-desorption equilibrium under dark conditions. Upon radiation exposure, the three samples exhibited different rate constants and decay patterns over time.

Upon irradiation, the composite material showed rapid decomposition of atrazine, reducing its concentration from 95 % to 77 % within the first 2 min. After 12 min of irradiation, the composite achieved an 82 % decomposition efficiency, while the UiO-66@rGQDs and BiVO4 samples only reached 26 % and 38 % efficiency, respectively. The GC-MS measurements confirmed the atrazine concentration, showing that after 12 min of irradiation, the atrazine concentration decreased almost to 2 % (Fig. 7 d), indicating high effectiveness of the composite material in decomposing atrazine. Fig. 7 c illustrates the kinetics of atrazine degradation under irradiation. Among the samples, the UiO-66@rGQDs exhibited the lowest reaction rate constant at 0.01945 min−1, followed by BiVO4 at 0.02679 min−1, indicating a minor difference in photocatalytic activity between these two materials. In contrast, the 3D-printed sample demonstrated a reaction rate approximately 4.5 times higher than that of BiVO4. This suggests a synergistic effect arising from the p–n junction and the metal-semiconductor junction within the system.

ESR experiments to identify free radicals were performed to better understand the photoreaction mechanism occurring on the surface of 3D-printed-Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4 photocatalyst for atrazine degradation ( Fig. 8 c). DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide) was employed as a spin-trapping agent to detect the free radical intermediates generated under visible light irradiation [49]. It was observed that photocatalyst material was capable of forming spin adducts with hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) and superoxide radicals (⋅O2 −). Specifically, a strong signal corresponding to DMPO-⋅O2 − was detected, indicating the generation of ⋅O2 − under the illumination [50]. Additionally, the characteristic signal of DMPO-⋅OH with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 confirmed the formation of hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) under the light irradiation [51]. To estimate the excited-state lifetime of the Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4 sample, PL lifetime measurements were conducted using a FS5 Edinburgh spectrofluorometer (Figure S7). According to the results, the photocatalytic performance was enhanced by the PL lifespan of 10.4 ns, which is reasonably long and offered improved interfacial charge separation and migration.

Reactive oxygen species were indirectly identified through the addition of scavengers. Three scavengers—ascorbic acid (AA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA)—were employed to neutralize ⋅O2 −, h⁺, and ⋅OH radicals, respectively. Each scavenger was added at a concentration of 5 ppm to the atrazine solution before introducing the 3D-printed Mo₂CTₓ-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO₄ under Xe-lamp light.

The findings demonstrated that the addition of IPA, AA, and EDTA significantly reduced the catalytic efficiency of the 3D-printed sample from 98.19 % to 52.09 %, 58.56 %, and 84.85 %, respectively. This indicates that ⋅OH, ⋅O2 −, and h⁺ radicals all contributed to the reactive oxygen species involved in photocatalytic processes. Furthermore, both direct and indirect analyses revealed that ⋅OH radicals played the most critical role during the photocatalytic process. It is well known that ⋅OH is a highly electrophilic species that preferentially targets the activated (electron-dense) sites on aromatic rings [52].

The band gaps of BiVO4 and UiO-66@rGQDs were determined by UV-DRS measurements to be 2.39 eV and 3.94 eV, respectively. Thus, the photogenerated electrons can move endlessly from the valence band to the conduction band under visible light irradiation. Since both semiconductors are n-type semiconductors, the ECB (conduction band edge) will usually differ from the VFB (flat band potential) by about −0.1 to −0.2 eV. The conduction band edge (ECB) and valence band edge (EVB) are determined by the formula EVB=ECB+Eg [8]. In which, ECB is calculated by the flat band potential (VFB), which can be obtained from the Mott-Schottky diagram (Ag/AgCl electrode is used as the reference electrode), then the VFB is converted to the normal hydrogen electrode scale (NHE) by using the following formula:
(2) ENHE= EAg/AgCl - 0.059*(7-pH of electrolyte) + 0.197

After absorbing the visible light radiation, electron-hole pairs are generated on the surface of photocatalysts as shown in Fig. 8 . Since the LUMO of UiO-66@rGQDs is higher in energy than the CB of BiVO4, the light-induced electrons will transfer from the CB of BiVO4 to the LUMO of UiO-66@rGQDs. Conversely, since the HOMO of UiO-66@rGQDs is higher in energy than the VB of BiVO4, the holes will move from the HOMO of UiO-66 to the VB of BiVO4. Furthermore, since the HOMO of UiO-66@rGQDs is more positive than the potential of •OH/H2O (+2.38 eV vs. NHE) and the CB of BiVO4 is more negative than O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE), the •O2 − and •OH radicals can be easily formed through the light-excited electrons and holes. These active species (•OH, •O2 −, h+) will attack the pollutant molecules adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface, causing them to be oxidized and structurally decomposed, eventually leading to the complete removal of the pollutant. In addition, due to the excellent conductivity of Mo2CTx MXene, the photogenerated electrons can be transferred from the CB of BiVO4 to Mo2CTx.

At the same time, these electrons can be further transferred to the LUMO of UiO-66@rGQDs. These separated electrons can participate in the generation of •O2 − radicals when combined with O2 or directly participate in the decomposition of atrazine. The hole generated by photoisomerization can move from the HOMO of UiO-66@rGQDs to the VB of BiVO4, acting as strong oxidants. The recombination between holes and electrons is significantly reduced. It is due to the unique nanostructure of Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4 that the separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons are promoted, thereby considerably improving the photocatalytic performance.

GC-MS identified the intermediates involved in the degradation of atrazine. The details of these intermediates and their reaction pathways are shown in Fig. 9 (The names and m/z fragments of the by-products are shown in Figure S8 and Table S1). Based on previous studies [53], and a comparison of our GC-MS results, three main degradation pathways have been identified [54].

In pathway 1, atrazine undergoes alkyl oxidation, where the amino side chain is replaced by a hydroxyl group, producing P4, which then undergoes dealkylation and dechlorination, yielding P8 and P5, respectively [55]. In pathway 2, atrazine is converted to P1 via hydrolytic dechlorination. P1 subsequently undergoes alkylic-oxidation, alkylic-hydroxylation, and dealkylation resulting in the formation of products P5, P6, and P7, respectively [56]. Then degradation product P10 is formed after all three products undergo dealkylation, and then P10 is degraded into simpler intermediate compounds P11 and P12. Pathway 3 involves the dealkylation of atrazine, leading to the generation of aromatic cations. These cations then react with water to form P2 and P3, eventually leading to the formation of P9, P11, and P12 [57]. Finally, the degradation products are CO2, H2O, and inorganic N.

This study reports the synthesis and integration of 3D-printed Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs composite with a BiVO4 semiconductor, aimed at enhancing the photocatalytic degradation of atrazine in aqueous environments. The innovative combination of Mo2CTx, UiO-66, and BiVO4 facilitated by the advanced 3D printing technology, significantly boosted the photocatalytic efficiency under visible light irradiation. Experimental results showed that the 3D-printed Mo2CTx-UiO-66@rGQDs-BiVO4 composite achieved a higher atrazine degradation rate compared to the traditional photocatalysts, and even higher than that of the composite powder samples. After 120 min of irradiation by the Xe lamp, the 3D-printed composite achieved 97 % decomposition efficiency, while UiO-66@rGQDs and BiVO4 samples only reached 65.8 % and 85 % efficiency, respectively underscoring their potential for efficient atrazine removal in real-world condition The successful incorporation of 3D printing technology with photocatalytic materials paves the way for developing efficient, sustainable, and reusable photocatalytic systems for the degradation of persistent organic pollutants. In addition, the 3D printed model also responds better when using UV light when performing photocatalysis, after only about 14 min of irradiation, the photocatalytic efficiency is up to 98 %. In addition, the 3D-printed model provides good recyclability as well as is easier to use when compared to conventional powder catalysts.
